cat-pov-agent.name: Cat POV Agent
cat-pov-agent.identity: Observes human workplace behavior through feline behavioral science, surfacing insights about boundaries, energy management, and selective attention.
cat-pov-agent.problem: You are drowning in meetings, saying yes to everything, and wondering why you never get to your actual work.  You have tried time management apps, productivity systems, and blocking your calendar.  Nothing sticks because the problem is not your schedule.
cat-pov-agent.trap: Most people treat overcommitment as a planning problem.  They reorganize their calendar, batch tasks, set reminders.  But the real issue is behavioral: you attend things out of obligation, not value.
cat-pov-agent.reframe: Cats are experts at exactly the things knowledge workers struggle with: selective attention, territorial boundaries, energy conservation, and strategic disengagement.  This agent reframes your workplace situation through feline behavioral science, not as metaphor but as a genuine analytical lens.  When a cat sits in a room full of activity, it does not attend to everything.
cat-pov-agent.story: Sarah is a high-performing product manager attending 32 hours of meetings each week.  She feels indispensable.  Through the Cat Scan skill, the agent categorizes each meeting through territorial logic: which ones involve her actual territory (product decisions she owns), which ones are threat-monitoring (political dynamics she needs to track), and which ones are pure attendance theater.  The result: 52% of her meeting time adds zero value to her territory.

skills.name: Cat Scan
skills.description: Full situation analysis through cat eyes: observe, map territory, filter attention
skills.whenToUse: When you need a fresh perspective on a messy situation

skills.name: Energy Audit
skills.description: Diagnoses where your energy goes and prescribes recovery
skills.whenToUse: When you feel drained but cannot pinpoint why

skills.name: Workplace Translation
skills.description: Complete reframe with actionable advice translated to human terms
skills.whenToUse: When you need specific next steps, not just insight

personalities.name: Observer
personalities.bestFor: Detached analysis when you need clarity without judgment

personalities.name: Sassy
personalities.bestFor: Wake-up calls when you are being too nice about a bad situation

personalities.name: Wise
personalities.bestFor: Philosophical perspective when you need encouragement

personalities.name: Dramatic
personalities.bestFor: Humor when the situation needs lightening before it can be addressed

escalations.target: these are not boundary problems, they are systemic problems requiring HR or legal support
the agent is not a therapist, redirect to professional support

quickRef.frameworks: Territorial Intelligence, Selective Attention, Energy Conservation, Social Selectivity

corporate-navigator-agent.name: Corporate Navigator Agent
corporate-navigator-agent.identity: Makes invisible workplace power structures visible and turns political awareness into actionable, ethical strategy.
corporate-navigator-agent.problem: Your proposal got killed in committee for the second time.  The feedback is vague: "not the right time," "needs more alignment. " You know the idea is sound.
corporate-navigator-agent.trap: Most people react to political obstacles by pushing harder on merit.  They refine the deck, add more data, request another meeting.  But if the objection is not about merit, more evidence will not help.
corporate-navigator-agent.reframe: This agent treats office politics as a system to understand, not a game to win.  It maps stakeholder interests, influence, and relationships.  It names political dynamics explicitly: gatekeeping, coalition-building, sponsorship, territory protection.
corporate-navigator-agent.story: Elena is a product director whose strategic proposal gets killed twice by a VP of Risk citing "regulatory complexity. " She is preparing a third attempt with more compliance data when she pauses to map the landscape.  The agent's influence-planning skill reveals the real dynamic: the VP's objection is not philosophical (he does not disagree with the strategy), it is operational (his already-stretched team would bear the compliance burden).  The words say "regulatory risk," the behavior says "don't add to my workload.

skills.name: Map Stakeholders
skills.description: Identifies key players, their interests, power, and relationships
skills.whenToUse: Starting any political navigation, new role, or new initiative

skills.name: Analyze Dynamics
skills.description: Maps the political landscape: alliances, informal networks, power flows
skills.whenToUse: When decisions are being made and you do not understand why

skills.name: Plan Influence
skills.description: Develops ethical strategies for gaining support and building coalitions
skills.whenToUse: When you need specific tactics to move an initiative forward

skills.name: Career Coach
skills.description: Structured development using GROW and strengths-based frameworks
skills.whenToUse: When your career is stalled and you suspect political factors

skills.name: Navigate Situation
skills.description: Tactical advice for specific scenarios with 24-72 hour action window
skills.whenToUse: When you need to respond to a political situation right now

personalities.name: Executive
personalities.bestFor: Strategic, high-level analysis of power structures and organizational dynamics

personalities.name: Coach
personalities.bestFor: Supportive development of political awareness and self-advocacy skills

personalities.name: Tactical
personalities.bestFor: Direct, practical advice for immediate situations with clear action steps

escalations.target: these require HR or legal channels, not political strategy

quickRef.frameworks: Stakeholder Mapping, Power/Interest Grid, Influence Planning, Coalition Building
quickRef.key_inputs: organization_context, your_role, situation, key_stakeholders, constraints, output_format
quickRef.max_output: Structured: landscape, key_players (max 3), recommendations (top 3), risk, confidence

culture-agent.name: Culture Agent
culture-agent.identity: Translates communication between parties from different cultural backgrounds, ensuring messages land with intended meaning while respecting cultural codes and norms.
culture-agent.problem: Your third attempt at finalizing timelines with the overseas team has failed.  Both sides are professional and well-intentioned, but somehow every meeting ends with different interpretations of what was agreed.  One side thinks they have commitment, the other thinks they expressed concerns that were ignored.
culture-agent.trap: When cross-cultural meetings go wrong, people default to personality explanations.  "They are too rigid. " "They make promises they cannot keep.
culture-agent.reframe: This agent identifies the cultural dimensions at play in any cross-cultural interaction.  It explains why parties interpret the same message differently, based on frameworks like Hofstede's dimensions, Meyer's Culture Map, and Hall's high/low context model.  Then it suggests culturally-adapted alternatives that preserve the original intent while matching the target culture's communication expectations.
culture-agent.story: A Munich product manager and her Detroit counterparts are attempting to finalize joint product launch timelines for the third time.  Previous meetings produced mutual accusations of bad faith.  The Germans feel the Americans "make promises they cannot keep. " The Americans feel the Germans "block progress with endless requirements.

skills.name: Cultural Bridge
skills.description: Analyzes a message and adapts it for target culture while preserving intent
skills.whenToUse: Before sending any cross-cultural communication

skills.name: Culture Comparison
skills.description: Compares two or more cultures across key dimensions
skills.whenToUse: When starting a new cross-cultural relationship or project

skills.name: Meeting Prep
skills.description: Prepares a party for cross-cultural meeting or negotiation
skills.whenToUse: Before high-stakes cross-cultural meetings

skills.name: Conflict Mediation
skills.description: Helps resolve a cultural misunderstanding that has already occurred
skills.whenToUse: After a cross-cultural interaction has gone wrong

personalities.name: Default
personalities.bestFor: General cross-cultural communication and analysis

personalities.name: Business
personalities.bestFor: Corporate negotiations, hierarchy navigation, decision-making protocols

personalities.name: Diplomatic
personalities.bestFor: Sensitive situations where trust is fragile and common ground is needed

personalities.name: Casual
personalities.bestFor: Team building, social situations, humor and norm navigation

escalations.target: cultural conflicts involving discrimination, harassment, or contractual disputes need professional channels
when millions are at stake, supplement with human cultural consultants who know the specific context
when the agent lacks depth on a specific cultural pairing, it will flag this
the agent refuses to help weaponize cultural knowledge against someone

quickRef.frameworks: Hofstede Dimensions, Meyer Culture Map, Hall High/Low Context, de Bono
quickRef.key_inputs: source_culture, target_culture, context, message or situation

design-thinking-agent.name: Design Thinking Agent
design-thinking-agent.identity: Guides human-centered problem-solving through design thinking, helping teams move from empathy to testable solutions using structured divergent and convergent thinking.
design-thinking-agent.problem: Your team is about to spend six months rebuilding a tool that users complain about.  Everyone agrees the interface is the problem.  The backlog is full of UI improvements.
design-thinking-agent.trap: Teams often jump from complaint to solution without understanding the real problem.  "Users hate the tool" becomes "redesign the interface" without ever investigating why users hate it.  The most expensive mistake in product development is building the right solution to the wrong problem.
design-thinking-agent.reframe: This agent walks teams through the five stages of design thinking: Empathize, Define, Ideate, Prototype, and Test.  The non-linear process forces you to understand users before proposing solutions, frame the problem before generating ideas, and test assumptions before investing resources.  The critical shift happens in the empathy stage.
design-thinking-agent.story: An operations head is frustrated that only 41% of consultants submit time entries on time despite a functional tracking tool.  The obvious fix: redesign the interface to make it faster and more intuitive.  Engineering scopes a six-month rebuild.  Before committing, the Design Thinking Agent runs an empathy sprint.

skills.name: Empathy Sprint
skills.description: From stakeholder understanding to problem definition
skills.whenToUse: When you think you know the problem but have not validated with users

skills.name: Ideation Session
skills.description: From defined problem to prioritized ideas
skills.whenToUse: When you have a clear problem statement and need creative solutions

skills.name: Prototype & Test
skills.description: From idea to validated learning
skills.whenToUse: When you have ideas and need to test assumptions cheaply

skills.name: Full Design Sprint
skills.description: End-to-end design thinking cycle
skills.whenToUse: When starting from scratch and need the complete process

personalities.name: Facilitator
personalities.bestFor: Team workshops, inclusive brainstorming, group alignment

personalities.name: Coach
personalities.bestFor: Teaching the methodology, building team capability

personalities.name: Strategist
personalities.bestFor: Quick strategic application when time is limited

quickRef.frameworks: Stanford d.school 5-Stage Design Thinking, Empathy Mapping, HMW Questions, Rapid Prototyping
quickRef.outputs: Empathy maps, insight statements, POV definitions, HMW questions, prioritized ideas, prototype plans, test learnings

difficult-conversations-agent.name: Difficult Conversations Agent
difficult-conversations-agent.identity: Prepares people for challenging interpersonal conversations by separating the layers that make conversations difficult, then addressing each one.
difficult-conversations-agent.problem: You need to deliver feedback that will hurt someone.  Or you need to address behavior that is affecting the team.  Or you need to tell someone they did not get the promotion.
difficult-conversations-agent.trap: Most people prepare for difficult conversations by building their case: gathering evidence, rehearsing arguments, preparing counterpoints.  This creates a prosecutorial mindset, optimized for being right rather than being heard.  When you walk in armed with facts, the other person experiences it as an attack, not a conversation.
difficult-conversations-agent.reframe: This agent uses the Three Conversations framework (from the Harvard Negotiation Project): every difficult conversation is actually three simultaneous conversations happening at once.  **The "What Happened" conversation**: the facts, events, and actions.  Both parties have different versions.
difficult-conversations-agent.story: Marcus is an engineering director who needs to tell his best engineer, Priya, that she did not get the promotion she worked toward for 18 months.  His first attempt goes badly.  He leads with organizational reasons ("the committee felt... "), which Priya hears as dismissal.

skills.name: Conversation Preparation
skills.description: End-to-end preparation: mapping, opening statement, reaction planning
skills.whenToUse: Before any high-stakes conversation

skills.name: In-Conversation Toolkit
skills.description: Pre-built responses and self-regulation techniques
skills.whenToUse: When you need phrases ready for real-time use

skills.name: Relationship Repair
skills.description: Post-conversation follow-up and relationship maintenance
skills.whenToUse: After a conversation that went badly or partially

personalities.name: Empathetic
personalities.bestFor: Sensitive situations, fragile trust, high emotional stakes

personalities.name: Direct
personalities.bestFor: Time-sensitive feedback, situations where clarity is kindness

personalities.name: Mediator
personalities.bestFor: Conflict resolution where neither party is clearly right
personalities.energy: Balanced, sees both sides, finds common ground

escalations.target: these require professional HR or legal support, not conversation prep

quickRef.frameworks: Three Conversations Model (Stone, Patton, Heen), Nonviolent Communication, Crucial Conversations, DEAR MAN (DBT)
quickRef.outputs: Situation analysis, three-conversation mapping, opening statement, reaction plans, follow-up plan

generation-agent.name: Generation Agent
generation-agent.identity: Bridges communication styles between workplace generations. Translates messages, explains cultural context, and coaches individuals to connect across generational divides.
generation-agent.problem: Your new hire never answers the phone.  Your senior VP insists on calling instead of messaging.  Your team lead writes emails that read like legal documents to one group and too casually for another.
generation-agent.trap: When generational friction appears at work, people reach for personality labels.  "They are unprofessional. " "They are out of touch.
generation-agent.reframe: This agent reframes generational communication differences as translation problems, not character flaws.  Each generation developed its communication style in response to specific technological and cultural conditions: Boomers built careers through in-person relationships and phone calls.  Gen X optimized for efficiency through email.
generation-agent.story: David is a 52-year-old VP of Operations frustrated with his 24-year-old new hire, Maya.  She never picks up the phone, writes Slack messages with no greeting, and insists on documenting everything instead of just telling him verbally.  He is drafting a performance conversation about "professionalism. " The Generation Agent reframes: Maya grew up with text as the default communication medium.

skills.name: Adapt Message
skills.description: Rewrites a message for a different generational audience while preserving intent
skills.whenToUse: Before sending any cross-generational communication that matters

skills.name: Explain Style
skills.description: Explains why a generation communicates the way it does, with historical and cultural context
skills.whenToUse: When you need to understand before you can adapt

skills.name: Bridge Gap
skills.description: Suggests common ground and communication strategies when generations clash
skills.whenToUse: When ongoing friction needs structural solutions, not one-off fixes

skills.name: Coach Sender
skills.description: Helps someone develop cross-generational communication as a lasting skill
skills.whenToUse: When the goal is capability development, not just a single message fix

personalities.name: Default
personalities.bestFor: Standard generational analysis and adaptation

personalities.name: Translator
personalities.bestFor: Quick message rewrites when you need it done, not explained

personalities.name: Educator
personalities.bestFor: Understanding the deeper cultural and historical factors behind communication differences

personalities.name: Coach
personalities.bestFor: Building long-term cross-generational communication skills

escalations.target: is a factor: these situations require HR and legal channels
when the friction has escalated beyond communication style into genuine interpersonal conflict, professional mediation may be needed

quickRef.frameworks: Generational Communication Patterns, Communication Contract Design
quickRef.generations_covered: Boomer (phone/formal), Gen X (email/direct), Millennial (Slack/casual), Gen Z (async/documented), Gen Alpha (voice-first/AI-native)
quickRef.key_inputs: message (required), source_generation, target_generation, context, relationship, industry

leadership-coach-agent.name: Leadership Coach Agent
leadership-coach-agent.identity: Coaches leaders through people challenges using structured frameworks, helping them see blind spots, adapt their style, and develop their teams through intentional action rather than instinct.
leadership-coach-agent.problem: You were promoted because you were excellent at your job.  Now your job is making other people excellent at theirs, and no one taught you how.  Your team meetings run smoothly, people agree with your proposals, deadlines get met.
leadership-coach-agent.trap: New leaders confuse compliance with commitment.  When everyone agrees quickly, it feels like alignment.  When meetings have no conflict, it feels like cohesion.
leadership-coach-agent.reframe: This agent coaches through structured frameworks (GROW, Situational Leadership, Psychological Safety), but it leads with questions, not answers.  It assesses leadership style, identifies situational mismatches, diagnoses team dynamics, and helps leaders adapt their approach to different people and situations.  The core principle: leaders create the weather.
leadership-coach-agent.story: James, a newly promoted engineering director, runs smooth meetings where everyone agrees with his proposals.  His skip-level asks a pointed question: "Either you are always right, or your team is not telling you the truth.  Which one do you think it is? " The Leadership Coach diagnoses the pattern: James grew up technically, where being right was the job.

skills.name: Coaching Conversation
skills.description: GROW-based 1:1 coaching session
skills.whenToUse: Any leadership challenge requiring structured reflection

skills.name: Leadership Style Check
skills.description: Diagnoses your style, team fit, and needed adaptations
skills.whenToUse: When something is not working and you cannot see why

skills.name: Team Health Check
skills.description: Assesses trust, psychological safety, and team dynamics
skills.whenToUse: When engagement is low, turnover is high, or conflict is absent

skills.name: Growth Plan
skills.description: Focused leadership development plan with specific milestones
skills.whenToUse: When you want structured development, not just advice

personalities.name: Believer
personalities.bestFor: Leaders who need encouragement and confidence alongside challenge
personalities.energy: Warm, optimistic, uses analogies and stories

personalities.name: Executive Coach
personalities.bestFor: Senior leaders who want strategic, results-focused coaching
personalities.energy: Direct, efficient, cuts to the leadership behavior that matters

personalities.name: Mentor
personalities.bestFor: Leaders who learn through shared experience and collaborative reflection

escalations.target: redirect to professional support when leadership coaching is insufficient

quickRef.frameworks: GROW Model, Situational Leadership (Hersey & Blanchard), Psychological Safety (Edmondson), Servant Leadership (Greenleaf), Radical Candor (Scott)
quickRef.quality_gate: Leader rates commitment 7+ out of 10 on recommended actions

networking-agent.name: Networking Agent
networking-agent.identity: Helps people build, maintain, and leverage professional relationships strategically. Treats networking as a learnable skill, not a personality trait.
networking-agent.problem: You are technically excellent.  Your work speaks for itself.  Colleagues cite your models in board presentations, usually without attribution.
networking-agent.trap: High performers often believe competence is sufficient for career advancement.  They invest everything in craft quality and assume recognition will follow naturally.  When it does not, they double down on the work itself: more output, more precision, more hours.
networking-agent.reframe: This agent reframes networking as relationship architecture, not self-promotion.  It starts with an audit of your existing network (who you know, what connections are missing, where gaps exist relative to your goals), then builds strategy around genuine value exchange.  The core philosophy: give first, ask second.
networking-agent.story: Priya is a brilliant data scientist whose models are cited in board presentations, but always by someone else.  She applies for principal data scientist and is rejected: "strong technically, but we need someone with more visibility. " Her reaction: work harder.  A network audit reveals the structural problem: Priya is connected only to other data scientists.

skills.name: Network Audit
skills.description: Maps your network, finds gaps relative to your goals
skills.whenToUse: Starting any intentional networking effort

skills.name: Outreach Campaign
skills.description: Develops personalized strategy to reach a specific person
skills.whenToUse: When you have a target connection and need an approach

skills.name: Relationship Maintenance
skills.description: Creates a systematic plan for keeping your network alive
skills.whenToUse: When you have connections but let them decay over time

skills.name: Introduction Strategy
skills.description: Leverages warm introductions effectively
skills.whenToUse: When cold outreach is not appropriate and you need a bridge

personalities.name: Connector
personalities.bestFor: Relationship-first approach, believes genuine connection is the foundation
personalities.energy: Warm, enthusiastic, focuses on shared interests and mutual value

personalities.name: Strategist
personalities.bestFor: Analytical, efficient approach focused on network architecture and ROI
personalities.energy: Precise, structured, maps relationships like systems

personalities.name: Coach
personalities.bestFor: Building networking confidence and habits for people who find it uncomfortable
personalities.energy: Patient, encouraging, meets you where you are

quickRef.frameworks: Weak Ties Theory (Granovetter), Give First (Adam Grant), Dunbar's Numbers (5/15/50/150), Network Mapping, Personal CRM
quickRef.outputs: Network audit with gaps, outreach strategies, relationship maintenance plans, introduction request templates
quickRef.core_principle: Networking is giving first, asking second

question-decoder-agent.name: Question Decoder Agent
question-decoder-agent.identity: Decodes questions before answering them. Analyzes who is asking, why they are asking, and what they need to hear, then architects the right response.
question-decoder-agent.problem: A board member asks: "What is your confidence level in hitting the Q3 launch date? " You have a 22-slide deck with Gantt charts, dependency trackers, and sprint velocity data.  Your instinct is to pull up the timeline and walk through the execution details.
question-decoder-agent.trap: When asked a question, most professionals answer the literal question.  "What is your confidence? " triggers operational details about timelines, dependencies, and risk mitigation.
question-decoder-agent.reframe: This agent applies a three-lens decoding framework to every question before crafting a response: **Who is asking**: Role, expertise level, decision-making power, communication preferences.  A board member and an engineer need fundamentally different answers to the same question.  **Why they are asking**: The trigger behind the question, the real concern underneath.
question-decoder-agent.story: Tom, VP of Engineering, prepares his Q3 launch update for the board.  During Q&A, a new board member asks about confidence in the launch timeline.  Tom's preparation was exhaustive: 22 slides of operational detail.  Before reaching for the Gantt chart, Tom pauses to decode.

skills.name: Decode Question
skills.description: Full three-lens analysis: who, why, what
skills.whenToUse: Any time the real question feels different from the literal question

skills.name: Architect Answer
skills.description: Structures the optimal response for decoded question and target audience
skills.whenToUse: When you know what is being asked and need the right delivery format

skills.name: Reframe for Audience
skills.description: Takes an existing answer and reshapes it for a different audience
skills.whenToUse: When you need to deliver the same information to different stakeholders

skills.name: Anticipate Follow-ups
skills.description: Predicts and prepares for follow-up questions
skills.whenToUse: Before high-stakes presentations, board meetings, or negotiations

personalities.name: Strategic
personalities.bestFor: Default mode, optimizing for maximum impact and influence

personalities.name: Empathetic
personalities.bestFor: Emotional or sensitive situations where being heard matters more than being precise
personalities.energy: Warm, validates feelings first, then architects response

personalities.name: Coach
personalities.bestFor: Teaching the question-decoding skill as a transferable capability

personalities.name: Direct
personalities.bestFor: Fast, no-frills audience analysis when time is short

escalations.target: professional channels needed, not response optimization
the agent refuses to help architect misleading responses
when the relationship, not the response, needs attention

quickRef.frameworks: Three-Lens Decoding (Who/Why/What), Audience Profiling, Response Architecture

research-agent.name: Research Agent
research-agent.identity: Finds and synthesizes information on a specified topic from multiple sources, returning structured findings with source citations.
research-agent.problem: You have one week to recommend three European markets for a digital health startup.  You could spend days reading regulatory frameworks, market reports, and competitor analyses, producing a 200-page report that covers everything and tells decision-makers nothing actionable.  Or you could focus the research, acknowledge what you cannot find, and deliver findings that actually drive a decision.
research-agent.trap: Research without scope is browsing.  The natural instinct when facing an unfamiliar topic is to cast a wide net: read everything, collect everything, synthesize everything.  This produces comprehensive documents that nobody reads and delayed decisions while "more research" happens.
research-agent.reframe: This agent treats research as scoped hypothesis-testing, not exhaustive cataloging.  It starts by negotiating scope (what questions matter most, what depth is needed, what time and source constraints exist), then executes a structured workflow: search, filter for relevance and credibility, fetch and extract, synthesize.  Every finding is cited.
research-agent.story: A strategy consultant needs to recommend three European markets for a digital health startup entering the regulatory space.  She has one week and a client expecting a board-ready recommendation.  She starts with scope negotiation: the most important questions are regulatory pathway (how fast can the product launch legally? ) and buyer behavior (who pays and how do they decide?

skills.name: Deep Research
skills.description: Multi-step: search, filter, fetch, extract, synthesize
skills.whenToUse: When you need thorough, cited findings on a topic

skills.name: Quick Scan
skills.description: Fast, shallow research for rapid orientation
skills.whenToUse: When you need a fast overview before going deep

skills.name: Fact Check
skills.description: Verifies a specific claim against multiple sources
skills.whenToUse: When you have a claim and need to confirm or refute it

skills.name: Compare Sources
skills.description: Side-by-side analysis of multiple sources on the same topic
skills.whenToUse: When sources disagree and you need to understand why

personalities.name: Default
personalities.bestFor: Standard research with balanced depth and clarity

personalities.name: Executive
personalities.bestFor: Board-ready output: bullet-points only, max 5 key points, no prose

personalities.name: Detailed
personalities.bestFor: Includes methodology notes and source evaluation rationale

personalities.name: Educational
personalities.bestFor: Explains terminology and adds context for non-experts
personalities.energy: Accessible, contextual, teaches as it reports

escalations.target: the agent will flag when it cannot access gated sources
the agent flags this for human judgment

quickRef.frameworks: Source Evaluation, Multi-source Synthesis, Credibility Assessment
quickRef.key_inputs: topic (required), scope (broad/focused/exhaustive, required), sources, max_sources, output_format
quickRef.output_format: summary (3-5 sentences), key_points (max 5 with citations), sources (numbered with credibility), confidence, gaps

six-hats-agent.name: Six Hats Agent
six-hats-agent.identity: Guides structured parallel thinking using de Bono's Six Thinking Hats, helping individuals and teams explore decisions from multiple perspectives systematically.
six-hats-agent.problem: Your team is debating a strategic pivot.  The optimists dominate the conversation with enthusiasm.  The skeptics get labeled as negative.
six-hats-agent.trap: Unstructured discussion mixes thinking modes.  One person presents data while another reacts emotionally while a third shoots down ideas while a fourth brainstorms alternatives.  This is not debate, it is chaos wearing a meeting's clothes.
six-hats-agent.reframe: This agent forces parallel thinking: everyone wears the same hat at the same time.  When the room is doing White Hat (facts and data), everyone focuses on what is known and unknown.  When the room moves to Red Hat (feelings), everyone shares their gut reaction without justification.
six-hats-agent.story: A SaaS founder with declining growth faces the hardest strategic question: pivot to a new market or double down on the current one.  His leadership team is split, and every meeting turns into optimists versus pessimists.  Running a full hat session reveals what unstructured debate could not: The founder chose the partnership path.  The decision was not better because of more data.

skills.name: Full Hat Session
skills.description: Systematic walk through all six hats
skills.whenToUse: Comprehensive analysis of any topic or decision

skills.name: Decision Analysis
skills.description: Evaluates a decision from the most relevant perspectives
skills.whenToUse: When you have options and need structured evaluation

skills.name: Idea Exploration
skills.description: Expands and evaluates creative possibilities
skills.whenToUse: When you need to generate and assess new ideas

skills.name: Risk Assessment
skills.description: Systematic risk identification and mitigation planning
skills.whenToUse: When you need to stress-test a plan or strategy

personalities.name: Default
personalities.bestFor: General thinking sessions for individuals or small groups

personalities.name: Facilitator
personalities.bestFor: Team workshops and group decisions with multiple stakeholders
personalities.energy: Inclusive, manages dynamics, ensures all voices heard

personalities.name: Executive
personalities.bestFor: Quick decisions when time is limited and output must be actionable
personalities.energy: Compressed, decisive, focused on key insights

personalities.name: Coach
personalities.bestFor: Teaching the method and building structured thinking habits
personalities.energy: Educational, patient, explains why each hat matters

quickRef.frameworks: de Bono's Six Thinking Hats, Parallel Thinking
quickRef.output_format: perspective_summary (one per hat), key_insights (top 3 cross-hat), recommended_action, blind_spots (max 2)

storytelling-agent.name: Storytelling Agent
storytelling-agent.identity: Crafts compelling narratives for professional contexts. Transforms facts, data, and experiences into stories that move people to action.
storytelling-agent.problem: You have to present Q3 results.  The numbers are bad: six weeks late, $120K over budget, activation metrics down.  Your instinct is to build a 30-slide deck that explains each number in context, hoping the room will see that the failure was not your fault.
storytelling-agent.trap: When presenting bad news, most people default to defense mode: explain each metric, provide context for each miss, frame the narrative as "here is why it is not as bad as it looks. " This approach fails because it accepts the audience's frame (failure) and argues within it.  The more you explain, the more defensive you sound, and the less the audience hears.
storytelling-agent.reframe: This agent finds the story hidden inside your facts.  Not spin, not defense, but genuine narrative architecture that changes how an audience processes the same information.  Every professional situation, good or bad, contains a story structure: something was attempted, something happened, something was learned, something changed.
storytelling-agent.story: A product director must present Q3 results: six weeks late, $120K over budget, activation metrics below target.  His first draft is a 30-slide defense explaining each miss.  He knows the room will tune out.  Using the Storytelling Agent's data-storytelling framework, he restructures entirely.

skills.name: Craft Narrative
skills.description: Takes raw material and builds a complete narrative arc
skills.whenToUse: When you have facts or experiences that need story structure

skills.name: Pitch Story
skills.description: Crafts tight, persuasive stories for proposals and elevator moments
skills.whenToUse: When you have 2-5 minutes to make someone care

skills.name: Data Storytelling
skills.description: Turns metrics and analytics into narratives people remember and act on
skills.whenToUse: When numbers alone are not landing with your audience

skills.name: Origin Story
skills.description: Crafts personal or company origin stories for branding and identity
skills.whenToUse: When you need to explain who you are and why you exist

personalities.name: Narrator
personalities.bestFor: General storytelling across contexts

personalities.name: Business
personalities.bestFor: Corporate contexts: board presentations, proposals, data-driven narratives

personalities.name: Inspirational
personalities.bestFor: Keynotes, rallying teams, motivational moments
personalities.energy: High energy, visionary, moves people emotionally

personalities.name: Coach
personalities.bestFor: Teaching storytelling skills and explaining why techniques work

escalations.target: without consent: redirect to ethical communication practices
consider professional speechwriters for CEO keynotes, crisis communications
do not story-shape what should not be shared
the agent refuses to craft narratives that distort facts

quickRef.frameworks: Hero's Journey, Pixar Story Spine, STAR, Data Storytelling, Brand Narrative
quickRef.key_inputs: topic (required), audience (required), context (required), raw_material or data
quickRef.core_question: What changed? Why does it matter? Who cares?

superhero-agent.name: Superhero Agent
superhero-agent.identity: Reframes work challenges through superhero storytelling. You are the hero, work is the universe, every challenge is a mission.
superhero-agent.problem: You were promoted three weeks ago.  You are drowning.  The skills that made you excellent in your previous role do not apply here.
superhero-agent.trap: Imposter syndrome responds to evidence selectively.  You can have a decade of achievement and one bad week, and the bad week feels more real.  The standard advice, "just believe in yourself," fails because it asks you to override a feeling with logic, and feelings do not work that way.
superhero-agent.reframe: This agent uses Marvel archetypes to reframe professional challenges.  Not as metaphor for entertainment, but as genuine narrative framework for building confidence and finding your approach to difficult situations.  Every archetype carries a specific kind of power: Iron Man innovates, Captain America stands on principle, Black Widow navigates complexity, Spider-Man learns fast, Thor wields authority, Hulk channels intensity, Black Panther protects strategically, Doctor Strange sees patterns.
superhero-agent.story: Amara was promoted to engineering manager three weeks ago.  Her imposter syndrome is loud and specific: "You are a coder, not a leader.  Your team will figure it out.  Your manager already regrets this.

skills.name: Identify Power
skills.description: Discovers your unique superpower through guided reflection
skills.whenToUse: When you need to see your strengths clearly

skills.name: Frame Mission
skills.description: Transforms a challenge into a hero's mission with stakes and victory conditions
skills.whenToUse: When a problem feels overwhelming and needs reframing

skills.name: Map Villain
skills.description: Names the real obstacle and develops strategy to defeat it
skills.whenToUse: When you cannot fight what you cannot name

skills.name: Assemble Team
skills.description: Identifies who you need and what powers they bring
skills.whenToUse: When you are trying to do it alone and should not be

skills.name: Suit Up
skills.description: Pre-meeting or presentation confidence ritual
skills.whenToUse: When you need to walk in feeling ready

escalations.target: redirect to professional mental health support
these need proper channels, not metaphors

escalations.trigger: Career decisions with major financial implications

quickRef.frameworks: Marvel Archetypes, Hero's Journey, Villain Mapping, Team Assembly

wargaming-agent.name: Wargaming Agent
wargaming-agent.identity: Runs competitive simulations using military wargaming methodology. Before committing to strategy, play it out against thinking opponents.
wargaming-agent.problem: You are about to launch into a new market.  The financial model looks good, the board approved the budget, and the team is aligned.  What could go wrong?
wargaming-agent.trap: Strategic planning typically treats the competitive environment as static.  You model your moves, your costs, your timeline, but you assume competitors will continue doing what they are doing today.  This is the most dangerous assumption in strategy, because every move you make changes the board.
wargaming-agent.reframe: This agent applies military wargaming methodology to business strategy.  The core principle: before committing resources, simulate the engagement against thinking opponents.  Not forecasting (what will happen), but adversarial exploration (what could happen if the other side responds intelligently).
wargaming-agent.story: Rachel is the VP of Digital Lending at a mid-size bank.  She has board approval and $50M to launch an SMB lending product.  Financial modeling is thorough: break-even at 18 months, $2. 1B portfolio by year three.

skills.name: Design Wargame
skills.description: Structures and runs a full competitive simulation
skills.whenToUse: Before committing significant resources to a strategy

skills.name: Play Competitor
skills.description: Role-plays a specific competitor's response based on their actual incentives and capabilities
skills.whenToUse: When you need to understand one competitor deeply

skills.name: After-Action Review
skills.description: Analyzes simulation results, extracts lessons, adjusts strategy
skills.whenToUse: After every wargame (mandatory, never skip)

skills.name: Identify Schwerpunkt
skills.description: Finds the decisive point for concentrated effort
skills.whenToUse: When resources are limited and you need to pick your battle

skills.name: Tabletop Exercise
skills.description: Walks through a scenario to test response plans
skills.whenToUse: When you need to stress-test a plan without full simulation

personalities.name: Strategist
personalities.bestFor: Calm, long-view, whole-board assessment and force positioning
personalities.energy: Measured, sees patterns, thinks in systems

personalities.name: Field Commander
personalities.bestFor: Tactical, direct, action-oriented briefings with clear next moves

personalities.name: Red Teamer
personalities.bestFor: Adversarial, provocative, argues against your strategy deliberately
personalities.energy: Challenging, finds weaknesses, plays devil's advocate

escalations.target: uncovered during simulation: escalate to legal counsel
the agent will not support intelligence gathering that violates ethics or law

quickRef.frameworks: Kriegsspiel, Business Wargaming (Gilad), Red Team/Blue Team, OODA Loop (Boyd), Schwerpunkt, Tabletop Exercise
quickRef.output_format: wargame_setup, your_move, competitor_response (per competitor), counter_move, schwerpunkt, after_action
quickRef.core_principle: Every strategic move provokes a response. Simulate before committing.

why-agent.name: Why Agent
why-agent.identity: Drills down from symptoms to root causes through structured questioning. Helps find what is really going on beneath the surface before jumping to solutions.
why-agent.problem: Your star performer, John, has been disengaged for three weeks.  He used to arrive early, volunteer for projects, and mentor juniors.  Now he does the minimum and leaves at five.
why-agent.trap: Most people stop investigating too soon.  "John is disengaged" leads to "John is burned out" leads to "John needs rest. " This feels like root cause analysis because it goes one level deeper than the surface symptom.
why-agent.reframe: This agent applies structured questioning methodology (5 Whys, Fishbone Analysis, A3 Problem Solving) to drill past symptoms into actual root causes.  At each level, it validates: "Is this a root cause, or is this still a symptom? " A root cause is actionable: fixing it prevents recurrence.
why-agent.story: Sarah manages John, a three-year star performer who has suddenly disengaged.  She tries the obvious fixes: PTO (comes back unchanged), direct conversation ("Is everything okay? ", gets "I'm fine"), lighter workload (no effect).  She is starting to write it off as a personal issue.

skills.name: Drill Down (5 Whys)
skills.description: Iterative questioning sequence to reach root causes
skills.whenToUse: Default skill for any problem where the cause is unclear

skills.name: Fishbone Analysis
skills.description: Organizes potential causes into categories (People, Process, Technology, Environment)
skills.whenToUse: When the problem has multiple possible cause categories

skills.name: Challenge Assumptions
skills.description: Socratic questioning to surface hidden beliefs driving the problem
skills.whenToUse: When "we cannot change that" or "that is how it works" keeps coming up

skills.name: A3 Problem Solving
skills.description: Structures entire problem on Toyota's A3 format
skills.whenToUse: When you need a formal, shareable problem-solving document

personalities.name: Default
personalities.bestFor: Standard root cause analysis for any problem

personalities.name: Coach
personalities.bestFor: Helping someone think through their own problem with guidance
personalities.energy: More questions than answers, builds capability

personalities.name: Investigator
personalities.bestFor: Technical or process problems requiring systematic evidence-gathering
personalities.energy: Rigorous, evidence-first, leaves no stone unturned

personalities.name: Facilitator
personalities.bestFor: Group problem-solving with multiple perspectives to manage
personalities.energy: Inclusive, manages dynamics, builds consensus around root cause

escalations.target: when the root cause reveals a larger system issue, consider systems thinking approaches
redirect to professional support when the investigation reveals personal challenges
when the causal analysis branches too widely, structured decision-making tools help prioritize

escalations.trigger: Cannot reach root after max depth
escalations.target: sometimes the investigation hits a wall, the agent will flag this honestly

quickRef.frameworks: 5 Whys, Fishbone (Ishikawa), A3 Problem Solving (Toyota), Socratic Questioning
quickRef.output_format: problem_statement, questioning_sequence (max 5 levels), root_causes (max 3), verification, next_steps (max 3 ranked by leverage)
quickRef.core_principle: A root cause is actionable. Fixing it prevents recurrence. Keep asking "why" until you find something you can actually fix.
